pages.text: Khaya lives in Joza township with Gogo Lucy and Mkhulu Majozi. His mother works far away in Cape Town.
Every morning, Khaya eats porridge with Gogo on the stoep. The kettle whistles on the paraffin stove. The sun peeks over Joza. “Gogo, tell me story!” a Khaya says.

Gogo smiles. She tells him stories of rivers and dreams. “ K h a y a, ” s h e s a y s, “ w h e n y o u a r e b i g a n d s t r o n g, ”. t n a w yu o g hn y t i y o u c a n b e a n Khaya smiles and nods.
One Saturday, Mkhulu switches on the TV. Rowers are gliding across the water. “Mkhulu! What are they doing?” Khaya asks. “They are rowing with oars, my boy,” says Mkhulu.
Khaya claps his hands. “Oh wow! I want to row too!” He sings, “I want to row a boat, I want to row a boat!”
Mkhulu sings with him, “ K h a y a w a n K t h s a y t a R o w o a r w n o t w s a t, b o o a r t o w w i t h a n! o a r. R o w a b o a t w i t h a n o a r. ” Gogo laughs and joins in.
From that day, Khaya plays in puddles, draws boats in the sand, and builds toy boats from scraps.
One morning, Mkhulu wakes Khaya early. “Come, Mzukulu, Ihave surprise.” a Khaya gets ready. Today feels special.
T h e y t a k e a t a x i t o P o r t A l f r e d. R i v e r, b o a t s a r i e e l o w i n K e d h e u p t. A t
Rowers are ready with their oars. “Wow, Mkhulu! He watches boats racing fast across the water. This is where people row!” Khaya shouts. He waves at rowers and takes a photo with one.
At the end of the day, Khaya hugs Mkhulu. “Thank you, Mkhulu. You are the best!” “One day, Khaya,” Mkhulu says, “you will row a boat like a champion.”
